title: Merck — Relationships
html:
Module 2: The Merck-ZoomRx Relationship

Governing Thought

ZoomRx's relationship with Merck is a decade-old engagement that has operated at moderate depth across multiple franchises but never achieved the deep embedding that characterizes the J&J partnership. The account is at an inflection point: Merck's restructuring into two BUs, the $3B cost-cutting mandate, and three simultaneous product launches (IDVYNSO in HIV, KN-689 in HNSCC, Enflonsia in RSV) create both vulnerability (budget pressure on renewals) and opportunity (new tracking work for new launches). The relationship's strategic ceiling is defined by one person — Rhonda Harp, who runs all of Merck Insights — and one blocker — the AI MSA addendum that prevents ZoomRx from selling its most differentiated capabilities.

Partnership Timeline

Year Milestone
~2015 Relationship established; early tracking engagements begin (Merck franchises since 2015 per Prevymis study records)
2015-2019 Steady engagement across oncology and vaccines. VAXELIS PET begins (4 waves through 2024). Prevymis HSCT tracking starts.
2019-2021 Gardasil PET active. Prevymis Kidney Transplant work begins (ATU Wave 1-2).
2022-2024 Keytruda PET (NSCLC) established as core engagement. HIV Pifeltro tracking begins. Enflonsia pre-launch research. HNSCC biomarker testing qual (Dec 2024-Jan 2025).
2025 Enflonsia PET + Digital Tracker launch. KN-689 HCP-Pt conversations. SubQ IO study kickoff. HIV RT sampling study. Keytruda NSCLC PET continues. Prevymis Kidney ATU Wave 2. Lynparza mCRPC PET.
2026 First global win: HIV DOR+ISL RT ($125K). Merck restructures into two BUs. Keytruda message testing proposals advance. Gardasil revisiting PET. IDVYNSO SFE tracking planned for 2H26.

Key statistic: The partnership now spans 5 therapeutic areas (Oncology, HIV, Vaccines, Transplant, RSV) with 10+ distinct project types delivered. However, depth within each TA is narrower than J&J — typically 1-2 project types per brand vs. J&J's 3-5.

Why Merck Works with ZoomRx (and What Keeps Them)

Pillar 1: Keytruda Depth

ZoomRx has built the most comprehensive external Keytruda insight library of any MR firm — spanning HCP-Patient conversations, biomarker testing qual, ATU baseline surveys, listening sessions, SubQ IO tracking, and NSCLC PET. Across 9 studies and 400K+ characters of parsed content, ZoomRx has developed cross-study pattern recognition (referral chain bottleneck, PD-L1 testing maturity, MDT dynamics, community vs. academic adoption divergence) that no competitor can replicate without years of longitudinal access.

Evidence: The cross-study synthesis (April 2026) identified 6 distinct cross-study patterns and 6 research gaps — the kind of consultative intelligence that elevates ZoomRx beyond data collection.

Pillar 2: Multi-Franchise Breadth

Unlike competitors who may hold one Merck brand, ZoomRx touches Keytruda (oncology), HIV (Pifeltro/IDVYNSO), Enflonsia (RSV), Prevymis (transplant), Gardasil (HPV), VAXELIS (pediatric), and Lynparza (mCRPC). This breadth — while less deep per brand than J&J — creates cross-franchise insight potential that Rhonda Harp's Insights team should value.

Pillar 3: Proven Global Capability

The $125K HIV DOR+ISL Global RT (March 2026) is Merck's first global ZoomRx engagement. This proof point unlocks the "global" positioning — ZoomRx can now reference a successful global Merck project when pitching global expansions for Keytruda message testing, IDVYNSO SFE, or future franchise work.

Growth Trajectory & BD Pipeline

The Three Growth Vectors

1. Franchise Deepening: Keytruda Indication Expansion

Keytruda is the dominant franchise but ZoomRx's engagement is still concentrated on NSCLC PET. The growth path: expand into HNSCC tracking (KN-689 launch monitoring), global message testing, ATU across tumor types, MSL tracking, and SubQ/QLEX adoption tracking. Each new indication or formulation creates a new tracking engagement.

Opportunity Est. Value Status
Keytruda NSCLC PET renewal + tumor type expansion $150-200K Must-renew
Keytruda Global Message Testing V2 $100-150K In pipeline (Apr 2026)
Keytruda ATU (new indication) $80-120K Opportunity
Keytruda MSL Tracking $60-80K Opportunity (harder sell at Merck)
Keytruda Social Listening [TBD] Opportunity

2. New Franchise Entry: HIV Launch Tracking

IDVYNSO launches May 2026. ZoomRx has already won the $125K global RT and has the SFE study design locked (2 waves, N=90/wave, 2H 2026). This is the fastest-growing franchise on the account.

Opportunity Est. Value Status
HIV RT retainer renewal + global expansion $125-175K High confidence renewal
IDVYNSO SFE Wave 1+2 (2H 2026) [Included in pipeline] Designed and locked
IDVYNSO NPP tracking [TBD] Opportunity (post-launch)
HIV MSL PET [TBD] Opportunity (if islatravir perception risk materializes)

3. Pipeline-Driven Entry: EyeBio, PCSK-9, Winrevair

Merck's pipeline investments are creating new franchise launch opportunities. EyeBio (ophthalmology) and PCSK-9 (cardiology) are pipeline assets that will need launch research. Winrevair (PAH) has a pricing workbook on file, suggesting early ZoomRx engagement.

Opportunity Est. Value Confidence
EyeBio launch services (PET + ATU + Launch KPI) $100-200K Low-Medium — new contacts needed
PCSK-9 launch services $80-150K Low — timeline uncertain
Winrevair tracking [TBD] Low — early signal only

Competitive Threats to the Relationship

Threat 1: BrandImpact — Historically Strong at Merck

BrandImpact has had a strong presence at Merck, particularly in tracking-type studies. They represent the most likely competitor for renewals and new wins.

Mitigation: ZoomRx's longitudinal Keytruda data (9 studies, multi-year patterns) is the moat. BrandImpact would need years of access to replicate the cross-study synthesis capability. Lead with institutional knowledge continuity in renewal conversations.

Threat 2: Spherix, InCrowd, DRG — Price-Aggressive Competitors

Merck's cost-cutting mandate makes the account vulnerable to price-based competitive displacement. Firms like Spherix, InCrowd, and DRG will compete aggressively on price for standard tracking work.

Mitigation: Compete on insight depth, not price. The referral chain analysis, MDT workflow mapping, and community vs. academic adoption patterns are examples of consultative value that cheaper competitors cannot match. Ensure every proposal includes at least one "insight layer" beyond data collection.

Threat 3: AI-Enabled Competitors

ZoomRx's AI capabilities (Dynamic Dialogues, Sagan, Patient Scribe) are a potential differentiator — but the Merck MSA AI addendum blocker means ZoomRx cannot sell these capabilities while competitors who don't have MSA restrictions might. If a competitor offers AI-enabled research at Merck before ZoomRx resolves the addendum, they could establish the AI positioning first.

Mitigation: Resolve the AI addendum immediately. This is the #1 BD priority after renewals.

Threat 4: Internal Merck MR Consolidation

The $3B cost-cutting and BU restructuring create a risk that Merck consolidates MR vendors — reducing from multiple to fewer preferred partners. If ZoomRx is not positioned as a cross-BU partner, it could be consolidated out of one BU.

Mitigation: Ensure ZoomRx has active projects in BOTH BUs (Oncology: Keytruda, Lynparza; Specialty: HIV, Enflonsia, Prevymis). The broader the footprint, the harder it is to consolidate ZoomRx out.

Merck's AI & Innovation Agenda

What We Know

Signal Detail Source
AI awareness at MR level Low — not aware of broader Merck AI initiatives AO Plan
Dynamic Dialogues pitch Pitched AI across teams using dynamic dialogs — not adopted more deeply AO Plan
Sagan follow-up quals Pitched for Keytruda — but blocked by AI addendum in MSA AO Plan
AI addendum status Working with procurement to get MSA addendum approved AO Plan
Cost-cutting context $3B cuts mean AI offerings must lead with cost savings, not innovation Restructuring news

Implications

Merck's AI adoption in MR is stalled at the procurement level, not the buyer level. Brand teams have been pitched and shown interest (Keytruda team on Sagan), but the MSA addendum blocks execution. Once resolved, position AI as:

  1. Cost reduction (fewer moderator hours, faster analysis) — this is the primary sell in a cost-cutting environment
  2. Scalability (global studies at lower per-market cost) — relevant for HIV global RT expansion
  3. Speed (faster toplines, real-time analysis) — relevant for launch tracking where time-to-insight matters

Rules of Engagement at Merck

Based on account history and the current environment:

  1. Lead with ROI, not capability. Merck is in cost-cutting mode. Every proposal must answer "what does this save or earn us?" before "what can this do?"

  2. Respect the BU split. Oncology and Specialty are separate worlds now. Do not cross-reference brands across BUs in proposals without explicit client permission.

  3. Protect renewals first. In a cost-cutting environment, the highest-ROI activity is ensuring every active project renews. Losing a $150K Keytruda PET renewal is worse than missing a $100K new win.

  4. Use the HIV global win as a proof point. The $125K global RT is ZoomRx's first global Merck project. Reference it in every conversation about global expansion — for Keytruda, IDVYNSO SFE, or any other franchise.

  5. Be ready for the AI conversation when the addendum clears. Have a pre-packaged demo or pilot ready (Keytruda HNSCC Sagan follow-up quals is the natural candidate) so ZoomRx can move fast once the blocker is removed.

  6. Build the Rhonda Harp relationship. She is the single most important person on the account. Every strategic conversation — capabilities showcase, new franchise entry, AI positioning — flows through her.

Relationship Health Assessment

Dimension Rating Evidence
Day-to-day project satisfaction Green Active projects delivering; no delivery flags
Strategic positioning Yellow ZoomRx is seen as a reliable tracker, not yet a strategic partner. The cross-study synthesis capability is under-leveraged.
Executive relationship Yellow Rhonda Harp introductory meeting held, but relationship is new. No champion at VP+ level yet.
Competitive moat Yellow-Green Strong on Keytruda (longitudinal data). Weaker on other franchises where any competitor could displace.
Growth trajectory Yellow 20% target is ambitious. HIV and Keytruda expansion are real growth vectors. But cost-cutting creates headwinds.
AI positioning Red Blocked by MSA addendum. Competitors could get there first.

Overall: The Merck relationship is healthy at the project level but under-developed at the strategic level. The gap between "reliable vendor" and "strategic partner" is the central relationship challenge for FY26.

Back to Accounts Hub | Home
